author: @AgentBenzo
text: I'm a fan of Luke's videos... they're typically always simple, very practical ideas that never crossed my mind, and can be used across a wide range of applications. Thanks again guys

author: @ryanlebeck259
text: I like how you took some time to show you using the tools. I think that a small series on proper tool use might be a fun winter project for you lot

author: @ciaransherry6021
text:
Good man Luke. As we say in Ireland, "Fair play to you", you got stuck in, got your hands dirty, and - Result.
Happy Christmas to you and your family.

author: @honeyflowbakery
text: Can't wait to see you flying down the road laughing histerically! Thanks for making my lockdown more bearable. Keep going!

author: @TheMan1510
text: Luke, please, please, please wear ear protection when you're grinding! I know far too many people who have damaged their hearing by not doing so, and I'd hate to see you do the same. Great video, by the way :-)

author: @rodneyjack3309
text: Good job Luke! Merry Christmas!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate! :)

author: @Moist_yet_Crispy
text: What is the motor from? Also great job and loved the vid!

author: @martynjones973
text: Nice one, don’t forget an enclosure, for safety and keep the weather out 👍👍

author: @jakattah
text: Wear ear muffs you want to keep your hearing!

author: @Twistedmetal-qe8kx
text: Really slick motor mount, nice job!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate! :)

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Looks really nice Luke, love the copper finish. You could build a housing for the motor and battery to include the mudguard and have it enclosed from any splashing in bad weather. The chain of course will have its clearance. Mounted underneath the motor might suffer from water splashing.
This is looking really good, once completed it's going to be a bloody ripper mate.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks for the tip mate! I'm definitely going to top mount the motor. I like this idea for the motor/battery to have some sort of housing to help it against the elements. Cheers mate!

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey glad to have an idea that might help

author: @stevejorgensen8123
text: you are doing a great job, keep it up

author: @bazofiascbr
text: I think I would rather have a rubber wheel on the motor, pressing against the top of the tire, behind the foot plate. Just because it should be a steadier connection in case of road bumps, and seems much simpler than chain or belt.

author: @docink6175
text: I agree the fabri9cation part is pretty simple for many, my problem is wiring it all together. it seems like most people who do these builds assume everyone knows what they are doing and glaze over the electronics especially iof its not a plug and play kit build

author: @stevvieb
text: Great idea on that draught excluder, I've always used old cycle inner-tubes for things like that but that draught excluder has much more dampening potential

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: If the draught excluder does fail I think I will use some old inner tubes that is a great idea! Thanks mate! :)

author: @robertpitt8418
text: EXALENT VIDEO WELL DONE  : )

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks mate! :)

author: @hempev
text: Isn't steampunk copper Robert's style of scooter?

author: @jamescunliffe9872
text: Just be wary of chainring compatibility issues with drive sprockets,geared bikes have different chain to tooth spacing! I don't know how powerful the motor is or if you're using the existing chain? Thinking reduction,bigger rear sprocket and small front? There's the option of direct friction drive to the tyre,instant reduction? Or fitting a geared rear wheel or hub transplant,depending  on spoke numbers! Ferrari stickers will make it instantly faster!!!;)

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: Hey Luke, what did that motor come from previously? I'm wondering about the torque that it has, I have been looking for a good torque motor with high rpm to build my grandson one of these type of scooters. Wondering what other items I could scrap to get the motor out. Cheers mate

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I think it actually came from an old scooter, but we have had this motor kicking around for a few years. I was actually going to use an old car alternator converted into a motor, but I didn't have enough batteries to run that for a long time. If you're interested in kits you can get a 24v 250w e-bike conversion kit from banggood . I think this would work really well. I hope this helps. Thanks mate! :)

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey Awesome

author: @endadalton
text: Great explanation, super excited for the next video.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks mate! :)

author: @tinkmarshino
text: Luke if you place it on the bottom you will run into it getting wet when you ride on wet streets.. and with it being thrown on to it instead of falling it can creep into some of the smallest place you can imagine.. Take it from me.. I have been a carpenter for 50 years now.. and water can get into places you would dream it could..

author: @charlesdickens6706
text: .......have you revealed the power rating of the motor? 250 watts perhaps?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I think I have? i will be talking about it in more depth in the next video. But it's rated at 12v 250w.

author: @richardsandwell2285
text: It is progressing really well, it should be fun taking it out on a test run.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I'm hopeful that I will be testing it in a few days. Super exciteing. Thanks mate! :)

author: @chuxxsss
text: Morning from Australia,  keep up the hard work.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Come on Aussie come on!!!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Good morning from the UK! Thanks mate. :)

author: @GoVertical17
text: inspiring, keep up the great work.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate! :)

author: @Wilksey37
text: Interesting to see how this progresses, as others have said, keep the motor up top to avoid getting knocked or getting filth in the motor itself from various hazards on the Great British roads and pavements (sidewalks for those on the the other side of the pond).  Would be interested to see the motor vs battery, there will be a fine balance between battery weight and run time for the motor.  Anyway, good work!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Yeah, I'm definitely going to be mounting the motor on top. I really like the idea of battery/weight and runtime of the motor. I will go through this in the next video. Cheers mate! :)

author: @Aerotech007
text: Looking really good Luke, i like the copper. Nice to see your confidence growing on every video you do, well done mate.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate, I don't know what made me choose copper lol, but I like it too haha. :)

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text: Hi luke, can i ask something. Well done on the copper finish, looking good. Are you planning to take this off road? if so maybe putting it on top would be better, to avoid uneven ground hitting the motor. As is wood going to be strong enough? if you make a metal/wood/metal sandwich that would be stronger. Anyway just some thoughts. All the best, kieron

author: @nicksgarage8295
text: yeah but how did you coat it in copper!!!!!!!!!!!!! like dude, tell us thx

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: Rob did a video not long ago.

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text: ....it's over a year ago . Other channels deal also with copper into aluminum .

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks mate! :)

author: @kenrowe167
text: Hi Luke. Nice motor mount. I would mount in on top, so you don't foul the motor when bouncing up and down kerbs. I know that's what I would have done when I was 20 ;-) Also, I would put a metal strainer plate underneath to spread the load from the bolts.

author: @Rpgamer50
text: Your vids are getting a whole lot better

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks mate! :)

author: @johnwilson3668
text: Right on .Very cool

author: @CraigDavies.
text: Are you going to put the  electric scooters through an M.S.V.A test so they can be used on the road for testing ?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I have not really thought about it, I think it depends on how well it performs at the end. But there will be upgrades to the scooter so it is definitely a possibility.  Thanks mate! :)

author: @William_Hada
text: Simple and functional design for the motor mount. I'm really liking it!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate! I really quite like this design. :)

author: @ffaubert1
text: Well done Luke! Excellent balance of knowledge and enthusiasm. Well done indeed.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate! :)

author: @serfer4219
text: cool work, i would try to put the motor mount on the wheel side of the rear bracket so it is out of the way

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Great idea, I think it would be possible to mount it onto the back brace where the back brake goes. I will have a look at this. Thanks mate!

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Mate I have been pondering over a motor mount and have to say I'm really really impressed with the final results . Well done 👏

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks mate! It seemed like this would be the easiest way to make one, to me. :)

author: @colouroboros9993

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey

author: @thornhedge9639
text: The copper is a nice touch.    Looks good Luke!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate, I really like the contrast of the colours! :)

author: @Tony-Elliott
text: Great video Luke

author: @Stuartrusty
text: Nice job Luke. Just a small point, beware of having the mounting holes of the bracket directly below the clamp bolt, it can make access and tightening more difficult.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks for the tip mate!

author: @lylehintz7275
text: Very nice looking job of it, keep up the good work!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate! :)

author: @il51diablo
text: I love watching your chanal but you can't imagine how angry I am to see that you don't protect your ears when you use the machines that make so much noise ...

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Lol, I am forever being told the same thing. But I am in mid 50's so I don't care, Luke has his whole life ahead of him.

author: @X02switchblades
text: very nice!!!

author: @qkitselectronics5415
text: Hi Guys, the thing you may find with your rubber door seal is, it may melt and possibly change its shape as it gets heated. Rubber roofing material will take the heat, there are probabaly other rubbers others could mention (keep it clean people), I could also be completely wrong. I  would want a solid piece, no gaps front to back, not to thick 4mm or so, just my 2 cents worth, good job getting this video out and making your ideas come to life. Keep your iron hot and your tip clean. Merry Christmas to all from Canada!

replies.author: @DiyEcoProjects
replies.text: good point

author: @michaelhicks6121
text: Great job sir!!! Cheers!! Keep up the great work!!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Cheers mate! :)

author: @barbufodor1186
text: nice. Any particular reason you chose to bent the "U" shaped aluminum "on the inside"?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Tbh there was no particular reason. It seemed like the easiest way to make something like this, to me. Thanks mate! :)

replies.author: @sweltermayo
replies.text: Gives it the artistic steampunk  look. Thanks for sharing 👍

author: @SkippedTilly
text: Great!.... now I've got to go and watch the vids that preceded this one. This looks like a fun idea. I live WAY out in the countryside and I'd love to be better able to visit my neighbors without the need for gasoline or it's equivalent in time. Cheers!

author: @ryanjames1501
text: That is a really great idea for a mount, love it. I would suggest top mounting rather than bottom mounting, in case of going over bumps or through puddles.

replies.author: @htmagic
replies.text: Yup, top mounting would allow water shedding easier!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks for the suggestion mate! I think I will go with the top mount, that way I can fabricate some sort of case for it to keep it protected. :)

author: @MaintenanceMouse
text: 🤯 Thank you!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks mate! :)

author: @imbabyface
text: Well done!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
